feedback.text: To whomever it may concern, I have been a gamer since I was very young and I was always passionate about this entertainment medium and everything that surrounds it. Growing up in the 90s and early 2000s I saw it transform from physical media to digital media. This is understandable that things change, however the hobby has been severely affected by large corporations that have seemingly been operating unchecked for way too long. These organizations, whether deliberately or not, are being increasingly hostile to their customers (consumers of media) by taking away their purchases/games. I can vividly recall when the Xbox One console was unveiled by Microsoft in 2013. It was meant to be the new big thing after the success of the Xbox 360 but what we were faced with was a scary precedent being set. Firstly, Microsoft elected to implement an "always on" system to the console requiring a persistent Internet connection. More importantly, they tried to implement a digital rights management system (DRM) for games that would essentially only allow a game to be activated once and bound to one users account. This would mean the physical disc purchased would essentially be useless and also the game would not be re-sellable or loanable. In addition, this would also mean that when (inevitably I suppose), Xbox One servers are shut down, all of those games would be useless as the discs would serve no purpose. Eventually, Microsoft walked both of these back due to the massive backlash and obvious fear that their console would not sell well. Of course, naturally, the latter ended up happening anyways as the customers lost faith in Microsoft. In more recent years this has become worse unfortunately. Microsoft and Sony have both launched disc-less consoles this current console generation which means that users are forced to buy digital only games that would inevitably disappear after PS5/Xbox servers are shut down eventually/potentially. It is worth noting that digital games are NOT cheaper vs their physical counterparts even though there is no manufacturing cost associated with them. Another entity that should be discussed is Valve which owns the largest PC game distribution storefront, Steam. While they are quite pro-consumer seemingly, all games on Steam are digital games so there is no telling what would happen if Steam were to disappear as now they have changed some of their wording to say that what we have been buying all along are not games, but licenses to games. Furthermore, Steam is now a graveyard of games that are abandoned by their developers and rendered useless after their servers are shut down due to a plethora of reasons. These are not always games that have not sold well but more often games that large corporations deem 'not profitable enough to support'. One such company is Ubisoft which is one of the biggest video game publishers currently. They are not a stranger to "online-only" games and their inevitable demise after said games are deemed not worthy enough to be kept around. Two very recent examples are the Crew and X-Defiant which were "sunsetted" (as the preferred term to killing their game) by Ubisoft with no alternative arrangement for the people that paid money for these games to continue to access them. This is how the initiative "Stop Killing Games" started and is currently fighting for. There is no shortage of games that are killed of with refunds never being offered to the consumers but even further, no way to preserve the game for history and the people after us to know about and experience. The only thing we are asking are for developers to give the communities the capability to keep the game alive after they stop supporting it. Of course it costs money to keep supporting online servers and it is okay to cut that support but we ask for developers to be more thoughtful and allow us to take care of preserving the game with no further costs inflicted to them. We ask for more rights as paying customers.
